channel.title: Varol Cagdas Tok
channel.description: Notes on cybersecurity, machine learning, software testing, and related topics.

item.title: Black-Box Testing Techniques
item.description: Black-box testing is a method of software testing that does not consider the internal structure of the system. Instead, test cases are derived from the external specification, which describes what the...

item.title: Test Coverage and Mutation Testing
item.description: After writing a large test suite, how do you assess its quality? How do you know if you are "done testing"? To answer this, we need **adequacy criteria**, which are metrics for measuring the quality a...

item.title: The Test Oracle Problem
item.description: In software testing, we dedicate significant effort to generating test inputs. However, a test input is only half of a test case. The other half is knowing what the **correct output** should be.

item.title: White-Box Testing: Symbolic Execution and Search-Based Testing
item.description: **White-box testing** is a testing approach that derives test inputs from the internal structure of the program. Unlike black-box testing, which uses only the specification, this method uses the code ...

item.title: Concolic Execution
item.description: **Symbolic Execution** is a powerful method for generating tests that cover all program paths. However, it has practical limitations. It struggles when code involves complex math, external library cal...

item.title: Fuzzing and Random Testing
item.description: While techniques like equivalence partitioning are systematic, another set of black-box techniques relies on a different approach: **randomness**. These methods are effective for finding unexpected bu...

item.title: Regression Testing
item.description: Software is not static; it changes constantly. Programmers fix bugs, add new features, and refactor code. Every time a change is made, there is a risk that it will unintentionally alter or break exist...

item.title: Introduction to Machine Learning
item.description: Machine learning has become a central technology of the 21st century, yet the core ideas behind it are rooted in centuries of inquiry into knowledge, intelligence, and learning. This article provides ...

item.title: Linear Algebra for Machine Learning
item.description: Linear algebra is the mathematical language of machine learning. From data representation to the mechanics of deep neural networks, its concepts are ubiquitous. A solid understanding of vectors, matri...

item.title: The Perceptron Algorithm
item.description: The Perceptron, invented by Frank Rosenblatt in 1957, is a foundational learning machine. Inspired by the biological neuron, it was one of the first algorithms to learn classification from data. While...

item.title: Linear Regression
item.description: Linear regression is a fundamental algorithm in machine learning and statistics. It serves as a building block for complex models and provides an interpretable starting point for predictive modeling. ...

item.title: Basis Functions for Non-Linear Problems
item.description: Many foundational models in machine learning, such as linear regression and the Perceptron, are fundamentally linear. They assume the relationship between inputs and outputs, or the class boundaries, ...

item.title: Introduction to Neural Networks
item.description: While methods using fixed basis functions can be powerful, they often struggle with problems where the complexity of the underlying function is not uniform across the input space. For instance, a func...

item.title: Function Approximation and the Curse of Dimensionality
item.description: In the field of machine learning, one of the most fundamental challenges a data scientist faces is model selection. With a vast array of models available—from linear classifiers and kernel methods to ...

item.title: Introduction to Deep Learning
item.description: After a period often referred to as the "AI winter," neural networks experienced a dramatic resurgence in the late 2000s, sparking the deep learning revolution that continues to shape technology today...

item.title: Recurrent Neural Networks and Attention Mechanisms
item.description: Many of the most important and challenging problems in machine learning involve sequential data—data where the order of elements is crucial. Examples are everywhere: time series like stock prices, sen...

item.title: Manifold Learning, Autoencoders, and Generative Models
item.description: In many real-world machine learning problems, the data we observe, despite being represented in a very high-dimensional space (e.g., the thousands of pixels in an image), does not actually fill that s...

item.title: Kernel Methods and the Kernel Trick
item.description: In machine learning, a common approach to modeling complex, non-linear relationships is to transform the input data into a higher-dimensional feature space using a set of basis functions. In this new ...

item.title: Probability for Machine Learning
item.description: Probability theory is the mathematical framework for reasoning about uncertainty and is a foundation for machine learning. Most machine learning algorithms are built upon implicit or explicit probabil...

item.title: Frequentist vs. Bayesian Statistics
item.description: Two dominant schools of thought provide the foundations for learning from data: the Frequentist and the Bayesian perspectives. While they often lead to similar practical results with large datasets, t...

item.title: Introduction to Linear Classifiers
item.description: Classification is the task of assigning a class label to an object based on its features. The **linear classifier** is a foundational tool for this task. Its core principle is to separate classes usin...

item.title: Support Vector Machines
item.description: In the landscape of classification algorithms, the **Support Vector Machine (SVM)** holds a prominent place. Developed by Vladimir Vapnik and colleagues, the SVM is a linear classifier distinguished b...

item.title: Model Selection and Estimating Generalization Performance
item.description: In machine learning, our primary goal is not to build a model that performs perfectly on the data it was trained on, but to build a model that **generalizes** well to new, unseen data. The ability to ...

item.title: The Linear Algebra of Machine Learning: A Roadmap
item.description: Machine learning algorithms, at their core, are transformation engines that convert input data into predictions, classifications, or generative outputs. These transformations operate on numerical data...

item.title: Vectors and Vector Spaces
item.description: A vector is an ordered list of numbers. Formally, a vector $v $ in $\mathbb{R}^n$ is an element of n-dimensional Euclidean space:

item.title: Matrices and Data Representation
item.description: A matrix is a rectangular array of numbers arranged in rows and columns. A matrix $A$ with m rows and n columns is denoted $A \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$:

item.title: Dot Products and Vector Norms
item.description: The dot product (also called inner product or scalar product) of two vectors $u, v \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is:

item.title: Matrix Multiplication and Linear Transformations
item.description: Given a matrix $A \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ and a vector $x \in \mathbb{R}^n$, the product $Ax \in \mathbb{R}^m$ is defined as:

item.title: Systems of Linear Equations
item.description: A system of m linear equations in n unknowns has the form:

item.title: Matrix Inverse, Linear Independence, and Rank
item.description: For a square matrix $A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$, the inverse $A^{-1}$ (if it exists) satisfies:

item.title: Eigenvectors and Eigenvalues
item.description: For a square matrix $A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$, a non-zero vector $v \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is an **eigenvector** with corresponding **eigenvalue** $\lambda \in \mathbb{R}$ if:

item.title: Eigendecomposition of a Matrix
item.description: The eigendecomposition (or spectral decomposition) of a square matrix $A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ expresses $A$ as:

item.title: Singular Value Decomposition (SVD)
item.description: The Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) of a matrix $A \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ is:

item.title: Application: Principal Component Analysis (PCA)
item.description: Given a dataset $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$ with n samples and d features, PCA finds a lower-dimensional representation that maximally preserves variance.

item.title: Vector Projections and Orthogonality
item.description: Two vectors $u, v \in \mathbb{R}^n$ are **orthogonal** (perpendicular) if their dot product is zero:

item.title: Application: Linear Regression and the Normal Equation
item.description: Given $n$ training samples $\{(x_1, y_1), (x_2, y_2), \ldots, (x_n, y_n)\}$ where $x_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$ are feature vectors and $y_i \in \mathbb{R}$ are targets, find a linear function:

item.title: Matrix Calculus: Gradients with Vectors and Matrices
item.description: Machine learning optimization requires computing derivatives of scalar loss functions with respect to vector or matrix parameters. Matrix calculus provides notation and rules for these computations.

item.title: Conclusion: The Linear Algebra Foundation
item.description: This series has explored the foundational linear algebra concepts required for understanding and implementing machine learning algorithms. The material was organized into four thematic units:

item.title: Introduction to Offline Reinforcement Learning
item.description: An introduction to offline reinforcement learning, exploring how AI agents can learn optimal decision-making strategies from pre-collected data without ever interacting with the environment. This post defines the core challenge of distributional shift and introduces the primary algorithmic paradigms—BC, TD3+BC, IQL, and CQL—that our research evaluates.

item.title: TD3+BC Algorithm for Offline RL
item.description: Exploring TD3+BC, a powerful yet simple approach to offline RL. We first detail the standard algorithm and then introduce our group's customized variant, which incorporates four key enhancements: dynamic hyperparameter selection, BC-weight annealing, state normalization, and episode filtering. We present experimental results from our paper demonstrating that these enhancements yield substantial performance gains (up to 6300%) over the default implementation, particularly on noisy, medium-quality datasets.

item.title: CQL and IQL Algorithms
item.description: Exploring Conservative Q-Learning (CQL) and Implicit Q-Learning (IQL), two sophisticated value-based methods that tackle offline RL's distributional shift problem. We detail their core mechanisms—pessimistic value estimation for CQL and implicit policy constraints for IQL—and discuss their performance characteristics as observed in our comparative study.

item.title: Benchmarking Offline RL Algorithms
item.description: Synthesizing insights from our extensive experimentation with BC, TD3+BC, IQL, and CQL across MuJoCo tasks. We present a quantitative comparison that reveals a clear conclusion: no single algorithm wins. The "best" method is highly dependent on dataset quality. We find that BC excels on expert data, while our enhanced TD3+BC and IQL show the strongest performance on noisy, medium-quality data.
